content:
{
      "id": "q1",
      "question": "What is the definition of a group in abstract algebra?",
      "options": [
        "A set with an associative binary operation, an identity element, and inverses for all elements.",
        "A set with a commutative binary operation and an identity element.",
        "A set with a binary operation that is closed and associative.",
        "A set with an identity element and inverses for some elements."
      ],
      "correctIndex": 0,
      "explanation": "A group is defined as a set G equipped with a binary operation that combines any two elements to form a third element, which is associative, has an identity element, and for which every element has an inverse."
    },
    {
      "id": "q2",
      "question": "Which of the following algebraic structures is NOT necessarily a group?",
      "options": [
        "A monoid where every element has an inverse.",
        "A semigroup with an identity element.",
        "A set of integers under multiplication.",
        "A set of invertible n x n matrices under matrix multiplication."
      ],
      "correctIndex": 2,
      "explanation": "The set of integers under multiplication is not a group because most integers do not have multiplicative inverses within the integers (e.g., the multiplicative inverse of 2 is 1/2, which is not an integer). The other options describe structures that meet the group axioms."
    },
    {
      "id": "q3",
      "question": "Consider the symmetric group S_3. What is the order of the element (1 2 3)?",
      "options": [
        "2",
        "3",
        "6",
        "1"
      ],
      "correctIndex": 1,
      "explanation": "The element (1 2 3) is a 3-cycle. Applying it three times returns all elements to their original positions: (1 2 3)^3 = identity. Therefore, its order is 3."
    },
    {
      "id": "q4",
      "question": "Let G be a group and let H be a non-empty subset of G. What additional condition is needed to conclude that H is a subgroup of G?",
      "options": [
        "H is closed under the group operation.",
        "H is closed under taking inverses.",
        "H is finite.",
        "H is closed under the group operation and under taking inverses."
      ],
      "correctIndex": 3,
      "explanation": "The subgroup test states that a non-empty subset H of a group G is a subgroup if and only if it is closed under the group operation and closed under taking inverses. Closure under the operation alone is not sufficient (e.g., the natural numbers under addition are closed but not a subgroup of the integers)."
    },
    {
      "id": "q5",
      "question": "Which of the following groups is cyclic?",
      "options": [
        "The Klein four-group V4",
        "The symmetric group S3",
        "The group of integers under addition",
        "The general linear group GL(2, R)"
      ],
      "correctIndex": 2,
      "explanation": "The group of integers under addition is cyclic because it can be generated by a single element, either 1 or -1. The Klein four-group and S3 are not cyclic (V4 has no element of order 4, S3 is non-abelian). GL(2,R) is not cyclic as it is infinite and much larger than a cyclic group."
    }
  ]
